title: What are Key Factors for Updates in RL for LLM Reasoning?
authors: Peidong Wang, Demi Wang, Xufang Luo, Jiahang Xu, Xiaocui Yang, Shi Feng
abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a promising framework for enhancing the reasoning ability of large language models. However, much of the existing work is guided by heuristic intuition, leading to divergent algorithmic choices, even contradictory ones that nevertheless report empirical gains. To better understand this phenomenon, we conduct a theoretical analysis of RLVR updates. Our study reveals that differences in off-policy degree, determined by the number of gradient steps per rollout, substantially affect the distribution of importance sampling ratios and their clipping behavior, thereby altering which tokens dominate the update. Building on this insight, we characterize gradient expectation as the central quantity governing update dynamics and analyze the roles of token probability, advantage, and importance sampling ratio. Motivated by these findings, we propose Adaptive Clip Policy Optimization (ACPO), which adjusts clipping boundaries across token groups according to the empirical variance of their importance sampling ratios. Experiments on 3B and 7B models across diverse reasoning benchmarks, spanning mathematical problem solving, tabular QA, and logic puzzles, demonstrate that ACPO outperforms strong baselines such as DAPO and CISPO. These results demonstrate that principled, analysis-driven approaches yield more robust and effective RLVR methods. Code is available in: https://github.com/Control-derek/ACPO

infographic.key_insight: RLVR update behavior in LLM reasoning is strongly shaped by off-policy degree and clipping dynamics, which determine which tokens dominate learning.
infographic.engineering_relevance: ACPO offers a practical way to stabilize and improve reasoning fine-tuning by adapting clipping per token group using empirical variance.
method: Analyzed RLVR updates theoretically through gradient expectation, token probability, advantage, and importance sampling ratio.
Showed that more gradient steps per rollout change ratio distributions and clipping, shifting update emphasis across tokens.
Proposed Adaptive Clip Policy Optimization (ACPO) with group-wise adaptive clipping boundaries.
infographic.evidence_signal: Experiments on 3B and 7B models across mathematical problem solving, tabular QA, and logic puzzles show ACPO outperforms strong baselines like DAPO and CISPO.
infographic.watch_item: Adaptive clipping depends on reliable variance estimates, so it may be sensitive to batch size, reward noise, and implementation details.
infographic.paper_title: What are Key Factors for Updates in RL for LLM Reasoning?

title: Extreme-Case Distorted Utility under Moment Ambiguity
authors: Zehao Li, Yijie Peng, Hui Shao, Chung-Piaw Teo
abstract: Many operations decisions under distributional ambiguity, from pricing and inventory to capacity and contracting, evaluate an action through a tail-sensitive distorted utility of an uncertain payoff and hedge against the least favorable distribution consistent with a few known moments; the resulting worst-case evaluation is the inner problem of a moment-based distributionally robust decision. We study this inner problem, the extreme-case distorted utility under moment constraints, for a locally Lipschitz utility that may be nonsmooth and neither convex nor concave together with a general, possibly atomic, distortion. Recasting the problem in the quantile domain, we develop a unified method that yields exact first-order optimality conditions and closed-form extremal values and distributions for both the worst and best cases, drawing on nonsmooth variational analysis. A central step treats the monotonicity constraint by isotonic projection onto the monotone cone, turning an abstract infinite-dimensional restriction into an inexpensive inner solve that scales linearly in the discretization. The method recovers and extends classical moment bounds through three examples: a range value-at-risk extension of the Scarf bound, GlueVaR distortions with a reward--penalty utility, and a capped incentive contract under conditional value-at-risk. As the inner oracle of a robust min-max decision, the characterization embeds directly in outer robust optimization, illustrated on a real capacity-provisioning problem for generative artificial intelligence inference where accounting for moment ambiguity lowers required capacity while preserving service compliance.

infographic.key_insight: The paper gives exact worst- and best-case distorted utility values under moment ambiguity by solving the inner distributional-robust problem in quantile space with nonsmooth optimality conditions and isotonic projection.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This provides a drop-in oracle for robust optimization in pricing, inventory, capacity, and contract design, helping engineers hedge against ambiguous demand while keeping computations scalable.
method: Reformulate the ambiguous utility problem in the quantile domain.
Derive exact first-order conditions and closed-form extremal values/distributions for both worst and best cases.
Handle monotonicity via isotonic projection onto the monotone cone for a linear-time inner solve after discretization.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports validation through three benchmark examples plus a real capacity-provisioning case for generative AI inference, where moment ambiguity changes the capacity needed to meet service targets.
infographic.watch_item: The method still relies on moment information and careful numerical treatment of nonsmooth utilities and distortion functions, so implementation details and discretization quality matter.
infographic.paper_title: Extreme-Case Distorted Utility under Moment Ambiguity

title: Factored Sparse Approximate Inverse Preconditioning via Spectral Optimization
authors: Francesco Brarda, Tianshi Xu, Vassilis Kalantzis, Rui Peng Li, Yuanzhe Xi
abstract: In this paper, we study value selection for fixed-pattern factorized sparse approximate inverse preconditioners. Given a prescribed sparsity pattern for a factor $G,$ we choose its admissible entries by optimizing spectral objectives of the congruent preconditioned operator $P(G)=GAG^T.$ This differs from classical sparse approximate inverse and FSAI constructions, which choose entries through algebraic Frobenius-residual criteria. For symmetric positive definite systems, the spectral target is a cluster near $+1.$ For symmetric indefinite systems, where congruence preserves inertia, we introduce a bimodal loss that drives positive and negative eigenvalues toward separated clusters near $+1$ and $-1,$ while penalizing eigenvalues near zero. To make these objectives practical for large sparse matrices, we derive projected Krylov support-gradients. Lanczos runs provide both a stochastic trace estimate of the spectral objective and a Ritz approximation to the exact gradient. We implement the resulting gradient through a detached Rayleigh surrogate: the Lanczos data are computed without gradient tracking and held fixed, while the backward pass differentiates only recomputed Rayleigh quotients with respect to the admissible entries of $G.$ This avoids differentiating through the Lanczos recurrence while returning a matrix-free gradient on the prescribed support. We also discuss a projected Kernel Polynomial Method rule as a finite polynomial comparison. Experiments on finite-element test problems show that spectral value selection improves fixed-support preconditioners, especially for symmetric indefinite saddle-point systems. We further demonstrate a graph neural network model for predicting admissible factor entries across related matrices.

infographic.key_insight: Choose the nonzero values of a fixed sparse factor directly to optimize the spectrum of the preconditioned operator, rather than minimizing algebraic residuals.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This can improve fixed-pattern preconditioners for large sparse PDE systems, especially symmetric indefinite saddle-point problems common in finite-element simulations.
method: Use a prescribed sparsity pattern for the factor G and optimize spectral objectives of P(G)=GAG^T.
For SPD systems, cluster eigenvalues near +1; for indefinite systems, use a bimodal loss that targets +1 and -1 while discouraging near-zero modes.
Estimate objectives and gradients with Lanczos-based projected Krylov support-gradients via a detached Rayleigh surrogate, avoiding backpropagation through the Lanczos recurrence.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports experiments on finite-element test problems showing improved fixed-support preconditioners, with particular gains for symmetric indefinite saddle-point systems.
infographic.watch_item: Lanczos-based gradient estimation is approximate and adds implementation complexity, so convergence and robustness depend on the quality of the stochastic spectral estimates.
infographic.paper_title: Factored Sparse Approximate Inverse Preconditioning via Spectral Optimization

title: Reasoning as Attractor Dynamics: Latent Memory Retrieval via Gibbs-Weighted Energy Minimization
authors: Kanishk Awadhiya
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are traditionally viewed as autoregressive generators. However, from the perspective of collective computation, they function as high-dimensional Dense Associative Memories that store complex reasoning patterns as latent attractors. In this work, we investigate the energy landscape of mathematical reasoning. We posit that correct reasoning chains correspond to deep, wide attractor basins ("flat minima") in the model's output distribution, whereas hallucinations manifest as sharp, unstable local minima. To exploit this geometry, we introduce a retrieval mechanism based on a Gibbs measure of the trajectory's spectral entropy. By sampling multiple reasoning paths and weighting them by their inverse energy ($P \propto e^{-βE}$), we approximate the equilibrium distribution of the associative memory, effectively ``relaxing'' the system into a robust solution. Empirically, this physics-inspired mechanism improves Microsoft Phi-3.5 performance on GSM8K by 5.38\% (84.7\% $\to$ 90.1\%), demonstrating that inference is better modeled as a dynamic settling process into an attractor basin rather than greedy next-token prediction.

infographic.key_insight: Mathematical reasoning can be viewed as settling into deep attractor basins in an LLM’s latent energy landscape, with correct solutions emerging as robust minima and hallucinations as unstable sharp minima.
infographic.engineering_relevance: A Gibbs-weighted multi-path inference scheme offers a physics-inspired way to improve reasoning reliability by approximating equilibrium retrieval rather than relying on greedy decoding.
method: Sample multiple reasoning trajectories from the model
Compute a spectral-entropy-based energy score for each path
Weight paths by Gibbs inverse-energy probability (P ∝ e^{-βE}) and select the most stable solution
infographic.evidence_signal: On GSM8K, the approach improved Microsoft Phi-3.5 accuracy from 84.7% to 90.1%, a gain of 5.38% according to the abstract.
infographic.watch_item: The method adds inference-time sampling and energy estimation overhead, so latency, calibration of β, and stability of the scoring proxy may limit deployment efficiency.
infographic.paper_title: Reasoning as Attractor Dynamics: Latent Memory Retrieval via Gibbs-Weighted Energy Minimization

title: Towards an Automated Reasoning Tool for Complexity Analysis of Automated Reasoners
authors: Louis Rustenholz, Manuel V. Hermenegildo, Pedro Lopez-Garcia, Alessio Mansutti, Félix Ridoux, Niki Vazou
abstract: We present the theory underpinning a complexity analysis tool (under development) that aims at automating tedious parts of the analysis of complex algorithms originating from the field of automated reasoning. Examples are given by super-exponential quantifier elimination procedures in real and integer arithmetics. Our tool implements the following pipeline. * Together with the algorithm to be analysed, the user (expert, e.g. the algorithm designer) can provide key metrics to track, and lemmas to improve the analysis. In pen-and-paper proofs, these correspond to the "non-tedious" and "creative" parts of the complexity analysis, that require human ingenuity. * The second step consists in the extraction of (generalised) recurrence equations. Here, we rely on a novel higher-order abstract interpretation technique, built on the concept of operator semantics. It enables (optimal) abstract compilation of symbolic programs to different kinds of purely numerical recursive representations, such as recurrence equations on interval-valued functions or numerical logic programs. * Finally, our tool solves the recurrence equations. We propose to go beyond the direct usage of computer algebra systems (CAS), and use pre/postfixpoint-based techniques to discover and verify candidate bounds on the solution. This approach makes use, in turn, of recent progress in SMT solvers, and can also be improved by techniques originating in termination analysis research.

infographic.key_insight: The paper proposes a human-in-the-loop pipeline that turns complex reasoning algorithms into solvable recurrence equations to automate complexity analysis.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It could make cost analysis of symbolic and automated reasoning software faster, more systematic, and less dependent on manual mathematical derivations.
method: User supplies key metrics and helpful lemmas.
A higher-order abstract interpretation step extracts generalized recurrence equations from symbolic programs.
Recurrences are solved with pre/postfixpoint reasoning plus SMT/CAS support to verify bounds.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract describes a theory and under-development tool and cites example applications, but does not report experimental results or benchmark evaluations.
infographic.watch_item: The approach still depends on expert-provided metrics and lemmas, so fully automatic analysis may be limited for difficult or poorly understood algorithms.
infographic.paper_title: Towards an Automated Reasoning Tool for Complexity Analysis of Automated Reasoners

title: ReNIO: Reweighting Negative Trajectory Importance for LLM On-Policy Distillation
authors: Chen Lin, Kedi Chen, Wei Zhang
abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) improves LLM reasoning by training a student model on its own generated outputs, but standard OPD treats all student-generated outputs (SGOs) equally regardless of their informativeness. We observe a consistent asymmetry in controlled filtering experiments: in both OPD and on-policy self distillation (OPSD), training only on incorrect SGOs outperforms training only on correct ones. Our further analysis suggests that models trained on correct-only SGOs tend to generate shorter reasoning traces and show weaker reflection behavior, while incorrect SGOs better preserve exploratory reasoning near the model's capability boundary. To exploit this signal without requiring full answer-containing rollouts, we introduce ReNIO, which Reweights Negative trajectory Importance for LLM On-policy distillation. By using the student-to-teacher probability ratio, ReNIO identifies pivotal tokens leading to wrong reasoning traces and aggregates their information into a normalized sample weight, inherently assigning larger weights to likely negative trajectories without observing the correctness of final-answer. Since Re-NIO only uses prefix-conditioned token probabilities, it preserves OPD's prefix training advantage over full-rollout reinforcement learning. Across both mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks, ReNIO improves both OPD and OPSD, with representative relative gains of up to 8.90% for Qwen3-1.7B and 10.00% for R1-Distill-Qwen-7B on mathematical reasoning benchmarks. Code repo: https://github.com/BDML-lab/ReNIO.

infographic.key_insight: ReNIO improves on-policy distillation by upweighting negative student trajectories that better capture exploratory reasoning near the model’s capability boundary.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It offers a prefix-only training signal that boosts reasoning performance without needing full answer rollouts, making it practical for scalable LLM distillation.
method: Compute a student-to-teacher token probability ratio along generated prefixes.
Aggregate pivotal low-ratio tokens into a normalized weight for each trajectory.
Assign larger training weight to likely negative trajectories without checking final-answer correctness.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports consistent gains over standard OPD and OPSD across mathematical reasoning and code generation, with up to 8.90% relative improvement for Qwen3-1.7B and 10.00% for R1-Distill-Qwen-7B on math benchmarks.
infographic.watch_item: The approach depends on reliable teacher-student probability ratios and may be sensitive to model calibration, teacher quality, or noisy token-level weighting.
infographic.paper_title: ReNIO: Reweighting Negative Trajectory Importance for LLM On-Policy Distillation

title: Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Sparse-Reward Search in Commutative Algebra
authors: Giorgi Butbaia, Paul Orland, Coco Huang, Davide Passaro, Lucas Fagan, Michele Tarquini
abstract: Applying machine learning techniques to solving long-standing mathematical conjectures can be particularly challenging due to their extreme reward sparsity. As an illustrative example, we consider Kalai's algebraic Hirsch conjecture and recast the construction of its counterexamples as a sparse-reward reinforcement learning problem on graphs. We propose a constrained options-based HRL framework with an equivariant graph neural network policy, which allows us to learn useful temporal abstractions for this task. We evaluate our approach over a wide range of degrees and demonstrate that it consistently outperforms classical RL algorithms as well as greedy search. By exploiting the hierarchical structure of the problem, we effectively provide a first-of-its-kind application of HRL to a problem in commutative algebra.

infographic.key_insight: The paper reframes constructing counterexamples to Kalai's algebraic Hirsch conjecture as a sparse-reward graph search problem and shows that hierarchical reinforcement learning can learn effective temporal abstractions for it.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This suggests HRL with equivariant graph neural networks can help tackle extremely sparse, combinatorial search problems where standard RL and greedy methods struggle.
method: Constrained options-based hierarchical RL
Equivariant graph neural network policy
Graph-search formulation of counterexample construction
infographic.evidence_signal: The method was evaluated across a wide range of degrees and consistently outperformed both classical RL algorithms and greedy search.
infographic.watch_item: Performance likely depends on careful constraint design and may not transfer directly to other algebraic search problems without task-specific graph structure.
infographic.paper_title: Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Sparse-Reward Search in Commutative Algebra

title: Verifiable Auto-Formalization of Mathematics Using a Relaxed Natural Formal Language
authors: Zhicheng Hui, Lihan Xie, Xingzhi Qi, Zhehao Li, Yingjun Lan, Qinxiang Cao
abstract: Auto-formalization aims to translate informal mathematical content into formal languages that can be processed by theorem provers. However, directly targeting existing theorem provers requires LLMs to bridge a substantial representational gap between informal mathematical writing and formal proof languages. This gap also makes semantic consistency difficult to evaluate. We address these difficulties by introducing a Relaxed Natural Formal Language (Relaxed NFL) as an intermediate target for auto-formalization. The Relaxed NFL is designed to remain close to informal mathematical writing: it preserves the usual structure of informal reasoning and allows partially specified expressions and propositions, without requiring their precise interpretation to be fixed at the auto-formalization stage. The remaining ambiguity and implicitness inherited from informal reasoning are resolved during a later elaboration stage, which transforms Relaxed NFL proofs into Core Natural Formal Language (Core NFL) proofs with formally defined semantics. The elaboration procedure combines rule-based transformations with LLM-generated heuristics, while maintaining verifiability through explicit constraints on each transformation step. The Core NFL is then used to generate proof gaps, namely verification conditions that must hold for the formalized proof to be correct. These gaps are discharged by LLM-generated proof scripts written in a domain-specific tactic language, which provides commands for invoking theorem libraries and domain-specific solvers implemented as part of our system.

infographic.key_insight: The paper introduces Relaxed Natural Formal Language as an intermediate, more human-like target for auto-formalization, then systematically elaborates it into verifiable Core NFL proofs.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This reduces the language gap between informal mathematics and theorem provers, making automated proof generation more practical, inspectable, and easier to verify.
method: Translate informal math into Relaxed NFL with partial expressions and statements allowed
Elaborate Relaxed NFL into Core NFL using rule-based transforms plus LLM-generated heuristics
Generate proof gaps from Core NFL and discharge them with LLM-written tactic scripts and solver calls
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract describes a verifiable pipeline with explicit constraints on each transformation stage, but does not report quantitative results or benchmark numbers.
infographic.watch_item: The approach still depends on heuristic LLM-generated elaboration and tactic scripts, so reliability may hinge on how well the system controls ambiguity and transformation errors.
infographic.paper_title: Verifiable Auto-Formalization of Mathematics Using a Relaxed Natural Formal Language

title: Beyond Penalizing Mistakes: Stabilizing Efficiency Training in Large Reasoning Models via Adaptive Correct-Only Rewards
authors: Jungseob Lee, Seungyoon Lee, Seongtae Hong, Minhyuk Kim, Chanjun Park, Heuiseok Lim
abstract: Training large language models to reason efficiently is a critical challenge. While integrating length-penalizing rewards into Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) aims to reduce verbosity, it frequently triggers reward collapse, severely degrading reasoning capabilities. Through a systematic evaluation of various reward configurations, we identify the root mechanism: GRPO's group normalization creates divergent advantages when incorrect answers receive continuous length penalties. Consequently, methods penalizing the length of incorrect answers are structurally prone to collapse under sustained optimization. Furthermore, restricting penalties exclusively to correct answers avoids this primary failure, but leaves the model susceptible to a stochastic collapse driven by response over-compression. To robustly prevent both failure modes, we propose ACOER (Adaptive Correct-Only Efficiency Reward). ACOER eliminates the structural penalty loop by isolating brevity bonuses to correct completions and prevents stochastic compression via dynamic budget normalization and control-loop penalty adjustments. Evaluated across diverse mathematical reasoning benchmarks, ACOER improves overall accuracy compared to the base model while reducing token generation by over 60%, establishing a fundamentally stable approach for efficiency-aware optimization.

infographic.key_insight: Length penalties on incorrect answers can destabilize GRPO, so efficiency training is safer when brevity rewards are applied only to correct completions and controlled adaptively.
infographic.engineering_relevance: ACOER offers a more stable way to reduce reasoning cost in large models, improving accuracy while cutting token usage by over 60% across math benchmarks.
method: Use correct-only rewards to avoid penalizing wrong answers repeatedly.
Add dynamic budget normalization to prevent over-compression.
Apply control-loop penalty adjustments to stabilize training over time.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports a systematic evaluation across diverse mathematical reasoning benchmarks, showing improved accuracy over the base model with more than 60% fewer generated tokens.
infographic.watch_item: Adaptive reward control may be sensitive to budget tuning and training dynamics, so miscalibration could still cause efficiency collapse or reduced reasoning quality.
infographic.paper_title: Beyond Penalizing Mistakes: Stabilizing Efficiency Training in Large Reasoning Models via Adaptive Correct-Only Rewards

title: Multigrid Training for Molecular Generation using Graph Neural Networks
authors: Zixuan Ling, Paula Mercurio, Di Liu
abstract: Deep learning has demonstrated significant success for modeling biochemical molecular systems, where inputs are commonly represented as graphs or 3D grids. A major challenge is that computational cost scales with resolution, making full graph/grid computation of molecular densities expensive and often unstable. We introduce a multigrid training strategy that leverages low-resolution optimization to accelerate learning at higher resolution through parameter transfer across discretizations. For graph molecular representations, we progressively transfer parameters learned from a coarse graph to a sequence of increasingly finer graphs via biased random walk upsampling. For 3D molecular generation, we voxelize the molecular structures at multiple resolutions, pretrain a coarse-resolution conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE), and initialize a fine-resolution CVAE by transferring shape compatible convolutional parameters from the coarse model. Numerical experiments on receptor-conditioned 3D Ligand generation show that multigrid training accelerates convergence and improves generalization compared to training from scratch.

infographic.key_insight: Train molecular generative models on coarse graphs or grids first, then transfer the learned parameters to finer resolutions to speed up and stabilize optimization.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This provides a practical multiresolution training recipe for expensive molecular generation pipelines, reducing compute while improving convergence and generalization.
method: Pretrain on a low-resolution molecular graph or voxel grid.
Upsample or transfer parameters to finer discretizations via biased random walk for graphs.
Reinitialize fine-resolution CVAE layers with shape-compatible weights from the coarse model.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical experiments on receptor-conditioned 3D ligand generation, showing faster convergence and better generalization than training from scratch.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on careful resolution coupling and compatible parameter transfer; poor upsampling or voxelization choices may limit gains.
infographic.paper_title: Multigrid Training for Molecular Generation using Graph Neural Networks

title: Scheduling Thoughts: Learning the Order of Thought in Diffusion Language Models
authors: Jiawei Xu, Minghui Liu, Aakriti Agrawal, Yifan Chen, Furong Huang
abstract: Masked diffusion language models decode by iteratively unmasking tokens, where the unmasking order defines an "order of thought" that strongly influences generation quality yet is typically chosen heuristically. We derive a tractable upper bound on the sequential decoding mismatch, measured by the Kullback-Leibler divergence and expressed in terms of the model's pathwise log-likelihood, with tightness under sufficient model expressivity. This bound induces a dense self-aware reward over ordered trajectories, casting order selection as a principled policy optimization problem with a frozen denoiser. We instantiate this idea as Self-Aware Scheduling (SAS), which learns a lightweight order policy using Group Relative Policy Optimization and applies seamlessly to both any-order and semi-autoregressive decoding. On Sudoku with 1B MDM, SAS improves puzzle accuracy from 82.0% (best heuristic schedule) to 91.8%, and reaches 97.5% with second-stage fine-tuning along learned trajectories. On mathematical reasoning with LLaDA-8B, SAS improves pass@1 on GSM8K from 64% to 76% and on MBPP from 39.5% to 41%, consistently matching or exceeding heuristic schedules across generation lengths and block sizes. Project page: https://jimmyxu123.github.io/SAS

infographic.key_insight: The paper treats token unmasking order in diffusion language models as a learnable “order of thought” policy, and shows that optimizing this schedule can substantially improve generation quality.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For practical LLM systems, scheduling is a lightweight control knob that can boost reasoning and decoding performance without changing the frozen denoiser.
method: Derive a tractable upper bound on sequential decoding mismatch using KL divergence and pathwise log-likelihood.
Convert order selection into a policy optimization problem with a dense self-aware reward over ordered trajectories.
Train a lightweight scheduler with Group Relative Policy Optimization and use it for any-order and semi-autoregressive decoding.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports strong gains on Sudoku and math benchmarks, including 82.0% to 91.8% puzzle accuracy on 1B MDM and GSM8K pass@1 rising from 64% to 76% on LLaDA-8B.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on learned scheduling and may require additional fine-tuning, so benefits could vary with model size, decoding setup, and training stability.
infographic.paper_title: Scheduling Thoughts: Learning the Order of Thought in Diffusion Language Models

title: VeriEvol: Scaling Multimodal Mathematical Reasoning via Verifiable Evol-Instruct
authors: Haoling Li, Kai Zheng, Jie Wu, Can Xu, Qingfeng Sun, Han Hu
abstract: Scaling reinforcement learning for visual mathematical reasoning requires more than generating harder questions: as data volume grows, the reward labels themselves must remain reliable. Yet existing data pipelines scale supervision while trusting the labeller, and policy-side methods assume the underlying answers are already correct. We instead treat scaling as a verifiable data-construction problem and decouple two axes before any policy update: prompt difficulty, expanded by route-specific evolution operators, and answer reliability, enforced by offline hypothesis-test falsification. We instantiate this as VeriEvol, an iterative framework with two extensible components: a type-aware evolution module that rewrites low-difficulty image-question seeds into harder, image-grounded prompts; and HTV-Agent, a verifier that accepts an answer only after multi-source counter-evidence has failed to refute it. The resulting verified data scales in volume, extends by adding evolution routes or verifier channels, and plugs directly into existing GRPO-style RL recipes. On a five-benchmark visual-math suite, scaling evolved SFT data from 10K to 250K samples raises the mean accuracy from 35.42 to 54.73; then, with backbone, SFT initialization, and GRPO recipe held fixed, VeriEvol adds a cumulative +3.88 over an un-evolved RL baseline, of which +1.82 comes from evolved prompts and +2.06 from the HTV-Agent verifier. We release the prompts, data, models, code, and the full verifier trace of every sample, so that downstream work can scale and audit the pipeline rather than only inspect its outputs.

infographic.key_insight: VeriEvol improves multimodal mathematical reasoning by scaling both problem difficulty and answer trustworthiness, using evolution plus offline verification before any policy update.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The framework is practical for training pipelines because it plugs into existing GRPO-style RL recipes while making the data-generation and verification steps auditable and extensible.
method: Type-aware evolution rewrites easy image-question seeds into harder, image-grounded prompts.
HTV-Agent verifies answers by accepting them only after multi-source counter-evidence fails to refute them.
Iterative construction produces verified data that can scale by adding new evolution routes or verifier channels.
infographic.evidence_signal: On five visual-math benchmarks, scaling evolved SFT data from 10K to 250K samples raised mean accuracy from 35.42 to 54.73, and the full VeriEvol pipeline added +3.88 over an evolved RL baseline.
infographic.watch_item: The approach depends on reliable verifier coverage and traceable multi-source evidence, so weak or biased channels could still let incorrect labels pass.
infographic.paper_title: VeriEvol: Scaling Multimodal Mathematical Reasoning via Verifiable Evol-Instruct

title: Uniform Sampling from High-dimensional Spectral Norm Balls
authors: Michael R. Metel
abstract: Motivated by an application in machine learning optimization, this paper focuses on the challenges of sampling a matrix uniformly from the unit spectral norm ball. It is proven that all singular values of sampled matrices converge to 1 almost surely as the matrix dimensions increase. This result provides the theoretical justification for a proposed simple sampling method applicable for large dimension sizes matching matrices found in modern large language models. Experimental results demonstrate both the convergence of the singular values, as well as the exact and proposed approximate sampling methods.

infographic.key_insight: Uniformly sampled matrices from a high-dimensional spectral norm ball become nearly "all-singular-values-equal-to-1" as dimension grows.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This supports a simple practical sampler for large matrix sizes, making spectral-norm-constrained initialization or perturbation more feasible in modern machine learning systems.
method: Prove asymptotic behavior of singular values for uniform samples from the spectral norm ball.
Use this limit result to justify a simpler approximate sampling procedure for large dimensions.
Compare the proposed approximation against exact sampling in experiments.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports experimental results showing both singular-value convergence and agreement between exact and approximate sampling methods.
infographic.watch_item: The approximation is justified asymptotically, so small or moderate matrix sizes may deviate from the limiting behavior.
infographic.paper_title: Uniform Sampling from High-dimensional Spectral Norm Balls

title: Closing the Loop: Formally Verified Law as a Reward Signal for Self-Improving Legal AI
authors: Armin Heydari, Torben Leowald
abstract: This article develops an architecture that creates a formally verifiable reward signal to train legal AI, adapting the LLM proposes, verifier disposes paradigm from mathematical AI to the distinctive demands of law. We present an architecture comprising LLM-driven autoformalization into a formal legal calculus extending Catala, a verification kernel, and explanation generation grounded in formal proof traces. For the computational components of law, the architecture provides provable correctness. For open-textured legal analysis, it provides structural guarantees: every required stage of the legal argument is addressed, argumentation is exercised at the correct stages and not omitted, and the deductive links between steps are valid. We demonstrate the architecture on procedural deadline calculations in German law, Commerce Clause analysis in U.S. constitutional law, and cross-jurisdictional sanction proportionality. We further show that the same architecture has a structural advantage for legal AI training: a deterministic external verifier supplies verifiable outcomes for legal problems and thereby closes the traditional reinforcement-learning loop gap in law.

infographic.key_insight: The paper turns legal reasoning into a trainable reward signal by pairing LLM-based autoformalization with a formal verifier, so legal AI can learn from externally checkable outcomes.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For legal AI systems, this offers a path to safer training and deployment by enforcing verifiable steps, valid deductions, and correctness for computational legal tasks.
method: Autoformalize legal problems into a formal calculus extending Catala.
Use a verification kernel to check proof traces and compute reward signals.
Generate explanations directly from formal proof traces to make reasoning auditable.
infographic.evidence_signal: The architecture is demonstrated on three legal case types: German procedural deadlines, U.S. Commerce Clause analysis, and cross-jurisdictional sanction proportionality.
infographic.watch_item: Open-textured legal judgment still depends on formal coverage of argument stages, so real-world performance may be limited by how completely the formal calculus captures doctrine.
infographic.paper_title: Closing the Loop: Formally Verified Law as a Reward Signal for Self-Improving Legal AI

title: Adam Converges in Nonsmooth Nonconvex Optimization
authors: Zijian Liu
abstract: Adam is one of the most widely implemented and influential modern optimizers. Why is it effective across different optimization problems in practice? This question arguably lies at the center of the optimization community over the last decade and has motivated a substantial body of work aimed at understanding its convergence behavior. However, existing studies have mainly focused on the convergence rate of Adam in smooth nonconvex optimization, which unfortunately does not adequately capture practical settings, since many real-world problems are nonsmooth, such as those arising in training neural networks. Thus, these studies cannot fully explain the popularity and empirical success of Adam. Recently, an insightful and powerful framework called Online-to-Nonconvex Conversion has opened a new way to analyze Adam for nonsmooth nonconvex optimization. Unfortunately, prior works along this line share two common limitations. First, all of them ignore the important bias-correction term in the original Adam algorithm. Second and more importantly, many of them require extra operations that are not used in Adam, such as a clipping step. Therefore, the convergence guarantee for the original Adam method still remains unclear. In this work, we present the first finite-time analysis for the classical form of Adam, i.e., with the bias-correction step and without further algorithmic modifications, and prove that a randomly scaled learning rate ensures a convergence rate of $1/T^{\frac{2}{13}}$ for nonsmooth nonconvex optimization. Moreover, our result provably applies to the modern heavy-tailed noise regime, which is closer to practice. Interestingly, our theory is established under the parameter choice $β_1=β_2$, aligning with the recent empirical studies.

infographic.key_insight: The paper gives the first finite-time convergence guarantee for the original Adam algorithm in nonsmooth, nonconvex optimization, including bias correction and no extra modifications.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This strengthens the theoretical basis for using Adam in practical deep learning settings where objectives are often nonsmooth and noise can be heavy-tailed.
method: Analyzes Adam through the Online-to-Nonconvex Conversion framework.
Keeps the classical Adam update with bias correction intact.
Uses a randomly scaled learning rate and assumes β1 = β2.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports a provable convergence rate of 1/T^(2/13) for nonsmooth nonconvex optimization, including the heavy-tailed noise regime.
infographic.watch_item: The guarantee is derived under the specific parameter choice β1 = β2 and a randomly scaled learning rate, so direct applicability may depend on matching these conditions.
infographic.paper_title: Adam Converges in Nonsmooth Nonconvex Optimization

title: The Fractal Neural Operator: Overcoming Spectral Bias in Chaotic Attractors via Prime-Harmonic Weierstrass Encodings
authors: Kanishk Awadhiya
abstract: Deep learning models, particularly Transformers and Neural Operators, exhibit a well-documented "spectral bias," effectively acting as low-pass filters that smooth out high-frequency information. While benign in fluid dynamics, this bias is catastrophic for Chaotic Dynamical Systems, where the underlying strange attractor is characterized by fractal geometry and infinite spectral density. We introduce the Fractal Neural Operator (FNO), a novel architecture that utilizes a non-resonant prime number basis to approximate continuous dynamical systems. Unlike geometric encodings ($2^k$), which suffer from spectral gaps and resonance, our Harmonic Weierstrass Encoder injects infinite spectral resolution into the latent space. We demonstrate that FNO extends the valid prediction horizon of the Lorenz-63 system to 347 Lyapunov times, exceeding state-of-the-art Reservoir Computing baselines by a factor of 2.3x. These results suggest that "chaos" is not inherently unpredictable to neural networks, but rather requires non-differentiable, fractal embedding manifolds.

infographic.key_insight: The paper argues that spectral bias in neural models can be overcome by using a fractal, prime-harmonic encoding that better preserves the fine-scale structure of chaotic attractors.
infographic.engineering_relevance: If effective, this approach could substantially extend long-horizon forecasting for chaotic dynamical systems beyond what standard Transformers and Neural Operators can capture.
method: Uses a Fractal Neural Operator (FNO) architecture for continuous dynamical systems.
Introduces a Harmonic Weierstrass Encoder based on non-resonant prime-number harmonics.
Claims fractal latent embeddings avoid the spectral gaps seen in geometric encodings like $2^k$.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports that FNO predicts the Lorenz-63 system for 347 Lyapunov times and outperforms Reservoir Computing baselines by 2.3×.
infographic.watch_item: The method may be sensitive to encoding design and numerical stability, so the claimed gains need careful independent validation on additional chaotic systems.
infographic.paper_title: The Fractal Neural Operator: Overcoming Spectral Bias in Chaotic Attractors via Prime-Harmonic Weierstrass Encodings

title: Look Light, Think Heavy: What Multimodal Chain-of-Thought Reasoning Can and Cannot Do
authors: Zhuoran Jin, Kejian Zhu, Hongbang Yuan, Yupu Hao, Pengfei Cao, Yubo Chen
abstract: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) has become a standard method for improving reasoning capabilities in large language models (LLMs) by eliciting step-by-step thinking, but its effectiveness in multimodal tasks remains unclear. In this paper, we aim to systematically investigate the key question: What can multimodal Chain-of-Thought reasoning do, and where and why does it fall short? To this end, we evaluate 12 multimodal tasks across perception and reasoning categories using both 14 non-reasoning models and 8 reasoning models. Our analysis reveals several important findings: (1) CoT is not a free lunch and should be used selectively depending on the specific requirements of each task. For perception tasks, CoT can lead to undesirable side effects, such as reduced performance in visual grounding and object counting. In contrast, it proves effective for reasoning tasks involving mathematical, scientific, and multi-image reasoning; (2) Compared to original models, existing open-source multimodal reasoning models often yield only marginal overall improvements, possibly due to an overemphasis on mathematical reasoning at the expense of broader capabilities; (3) Visual reasoning remains a key bottleneck for current multimodal CoT, as models exhibit a Look Light, Think Heavy pattern where verbal reflection rises and falls during reasoning, whereas visual reflection consistently diminishes. These findings suggest that while multimodal CoT handles verbal reflection relatively well, it lacks the ability to maintain deep visual introspection throughout the reasoning process.

infographic.key_insight: Multimodal chain-of-thought helps reasoning-heavy tasks, but can hurt perception tasks and does not reliably sustain visual introspection.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For multimodal systems, CoT should be enabled selectively by task type rather than used universally, because it may improve benchmark reasoning while degrading grounding or counting performance.
method: Evaluated 12 multimodal tasks across perception and reasoning categories.
Compared 14 non-reasoning models with 8 reasoning models.
Analyzed CoT behavior in terms of verbal vs. visual reflection during reasoning.
infographic.evidence_signal: The study reports benchmark evaluation across 12 tasks and 22 models, finding strong gains mainly on mathematical, scientific, and multi-image reasoning, but weaker or negative effects on perception tasks.
infographic.watch_item: Open-source multimodal reasoning models may show only marginal overall gains, so applying CoT without task-specific validation can introduce regression in visual grounding and object counting.
infographic.paper_title: Look Light, Think Heavy: What Multimodal Chain-of-Thought Reasoning Can and Cannot Do

title: Sharp Sobolev Sandwich and Approximation Rates of Radon-Domain $L^p$ Ridge Integral Spaces for ReLU$^k$ Networks
authors: Juncai He, Zitong Tian
abstract: We develop the $L^p$ space and approximation theory for shallow neural networks with $\mathrm{ReLU}^k$ activations. The central object is the Radon-domain $L^p$ space $\mathcal{R}L^p_k(Ω)$ containing all functions on a bounded domain $Ω$ that admit a ridge integral representation whose coefficient density belongs to $L^p$ in the Radon domain. In the Hilbert case $p=2$, we prove by elementary Fourier analysis that this space recovers the critical Sobolev space $H^{k+(d+1)/2}(Ω)$. For general $1<p<\infty$, the identity becomes a Sobolev sandwich. The sharp gap of each side is exactly the Seeger--Sogge--Stein loss for the Radon transform as a Fourier integral operator. This also clarifies how the activation regularity and Radon back-projection jointly produce the regularity. As an application, we discretize the integral representation using a deterministic interpolation skeleton plus uniform sampling. This yields high-probability $L^p$ approximation rates and the optimal Hilbert rate $O\!\big(n^{-\frac12-\frac{2k+1}{2d}}\big)$ at $p=2$ for linearized neural networks.

infographic.key_insight: For shallow ReLU^k networks, the paper identifies the exact function space behind ridge-integral representations: in L2 it matches a critical Sobolev space, and in Lp it sits in a sharp Sobolev sandwich whose gap is governed by Radon-transform smoothing/loss.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This gives a principled way to predict approximation quality and sample complexity for linearized neural networks, especially when choosing activation smoothness and target regularity in high-dimensional settings.
method: Define a Radon-domain L^p space for coefficient densities in ridge-integral representations of ReLU^k networks.
Use elementary Fourier analysis to prove the L2 identity with H^{k+(d+1)/2}(Ω) and establish the sharp Sobolev sandwich for 1<p<∞.
Discretize the integral model with a deterministic interpolation skeleton plus uniform sampling to obtain high-probability approximation bounds.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports theoretical proofs and a discretization analysis that yields high-probability L^p rates, including the optimal Hilbert-space rate O(n^{-1/2-(2k+1)/(2d)}) at p=2.
infographic.watch_item: The sharp results rely on bounded-domain assumptions and Radon-transform operator theory, so practical performance may depend on how well a finite-width implementation approximates the continuous ridge-integral model.
infographic.paper_title: Sharp Sobolev Sandwich and Approximation Rates of Radon-Domain $L^p$ Ridge Integral Spaces for ReLU$^k$ Networks

title: Convergence of Gradient Descent for General Neural Network Architectures Beyond the NTK Regime
authors: Yuqing Wang
abstract: Training dynamics is central to understanding neural networks, yet its theoretical analysis remains difficult even for simple architectures and becomes substantially more challenging for general modern architectures. In this paper, we propose a convergence framework for analyzing gradient descent (GD) dynamics under a broad family of neural network architectures and datasets beyond the neural tangent kernel (NTK) regime. The framework is formulated at the level of network blocks and covers architectures including pre-normalized multi-layer transformers. More precisely, under mild assumptions, we prove that for almost all initializations, GD with regular learning rates converges to the neighbourhood of a stationary point. This is mainly proved by establishing an iterate-dependent PL-type inequality through analyticity and measure-zero arguments, and by proving Lipschitz smoothness along the GD trajectory through polynomial generalized smoothness and a local relaxed dissipative condition. We further interpret the theorem under Xavier initialization and practical architectural scaling, showing that the learning rate scale depends on the depth and effective bottleneck dimensions rather than the largest width. Finally, we derive structural nondegeneracy implications for residual connections and function composition, and provide a generic characterization of global minimizers within our framework.

infographic.key_insight: The paper proves that gradient descent can converge near stationary points for broad neural network architectures beyond the NTK regime by combining analyticity-based PL inequalities with trajectory-dependent smoothness.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It gives practical learning-rate scaling guidance for modern architectures like pre-normalized transformers, emphasizing depth and bottleneck dimensions over the largest width.
method: Establish an iterate-dependent PL-type inequality using analyticity and measure-zero arguments.
Prove Lipschitz smoothness along the GD path via polynomial generalized smoothness and a local relaxed dissipative condition.
Interpret the theory under Xavier initialization and architectural scaling to derive learning-rate and nondegeneracy implications.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports a theoretical convergence framework and proofs under mild assumptions, but no empirical dataset benchmark or quantitative experiment results.
infographic.watch_item: The guarantees are asymptotic and assumption-driven, so applicability depends on whether the architecture and initialization satisfy the stated analyticity, smoothness, and genericity conditions.
infographic.paper_title: Convergence of Gradient Descent for General Neural Network Architectures Beyond the NTK Regime

title: New Bounds for the Last Iterate of the Stochastic subGradient Method
authors: Guglielmo Beretta, Tommaso Cesari, Roberto Colomboni, Andrea Paudice
abstract: We study the last iterate of the stochastic subgradient method for one-dimensional convex Lipschitz objectives. For a fixed horizon $n$, we consider the standard fixed stepsizes $η=Θ(1/\sqrt n)$. We prove that, for such stepsize policies, under additive i.i.d. subgradient noise with uniformly bounded variance, the last iterate features an optimization error of order $1/\sqrt n$, thereby removing the extra $(\log n)$ factor present in existing generic bounds. On the other hand, we show that without the i.i.d. assumption, the optimization error can be of order $(\log n)/\sqrt n$. Thus, under the uniformly bounded variance assumption alone, the last iterate of SsGM is suboptimal even in dimension one, resolving negatively an open problem posed in Koren and Segal, COLT, 2020.

infographic.key_insight: For one-dimensional convex Lipschitz optimization, the stochastic subgradient method’s last iterate achieves the optimal O(1/√n) error with standard fixed stepsizes under i.i.d. bounded-variance noise, but can degrade to (log n)/√n without the i.i.d. assumption.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This result clarifies when simple fixed-step stochastic subgradient schemes are safe to use in noisy optimization pipelines and when additional assumptions or averaging are needed to avoid hidden logarithmic slowdowns.
method: Analyze the last iterate of SsGM for fixed horizon n with stepsize η=Θ(1/√n).
Prove an O(1/√n) optimization-error bound under additive i.i.d. subgradient noise with uniformly bounded variance.
Construct a counterexample showing (log n)/√n behavior when the noise is not i.i.d.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports worst-case optimization-error rates for the last iterate, comparing the improved O(1/√n) guarantee against a counterexample with (log n)/√n scaling.
infographic.watch_item: The favorable rate depends on the i.i.d. bounded-variance noise model; relaxing independence can reintroduce a logarithmic penalty even in dimension one.
infographic.paper_title: New Bounds for the Last Iterate of the Stochastic subGradient Method
